speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May the 3rd, 1995. The survivor is Godel Silber. And Judy Schwartz is the interviewer in Toronto, Canada. And the language is English. The date is May 3rd, 1995. The survivor is Mr. Godel Silber. I am the interviewer Judy Schwartz. J-U-D-Y S-C-H-W-A-R-T-Z Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Godel Silber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-O-D-E-L S-I-L-B-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Do you have any other names or nicknames that you like to be called by? OK. Can I have your birth date, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: June the 15th, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that means your age is--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born in what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ciechanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-- Ciechanow-- H-O-N-- N-O with a dash and-- and a a W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland. Thank you very much. I am Judy Schwartz interviewing in Hamilton, Ontario, Canada on May the 3rd, 1995. Mr. Silber can you tell me what Ciechanow, Poland was like before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews?

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of a population of how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city, [INAUDIBLE] Polish, with gentiles figured in, 30,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: 30,000 altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of gentiles around the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- so was it a major city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Major city, big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- was it far from-- where was the closest major--

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: 90 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: 90. Could you describe the Jewish community? What organizations? How religious? Could you explain that to us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a Talmud Torah. We had a mikveh. We had a big shul. We had a beis midrash and [INAUDIBLE], you know, little things-- little-- little like-- every-- every one had its own rabbi around the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go to your home. You had parents. And how many siblings? How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all together with me, 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had 10-- brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 altogether.

speaker: interviewee
text: Altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Real estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he in real estate with others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not the real estate like here. He had a few houses inherited and collect rent.

speaker: interviewer
text: He collected.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Was houselady.

speaker: interviewer
text: She looked after the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain what it was like to live in the town? Were the relationships between the Jewish population and the non-Jewish-- was there any relationship? Was it good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard. Very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are lots of anti-Semitic incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- do you remember any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't grow in the Polish territory. We had like-- it was not  a ghetto like blocked out-- blocked in.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had like-- like Jewish section. Over there we were 90% Jews. And the gentiles that were over there, they-- they didn't bother us because they weren't the majority. Or when we went out, let's see, a half a kilometer away, you were not safe. We went sometimes like Mr. [? Derrick ?] mentioned the Blue Jays. When heard of Blue Jays, we were young children. We wanted to go look at the sports too. So we had-- we had some sport for soccer like you caught with the foot. So when we went over there-- when we lost, it was no good. When we won it was no good. Always, you know, always, that was in every city, they had a-- a church. And all the churches mostly in Poland were on top of a little mountain. So they went over there and throw stone on us. So like I-- I went there half an hour early when I wanted to take a look at a score, I couldn't stay [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Was your family a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mizrahi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mizrahi. So you observed many rituals in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a public school or a cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went just to cheder. You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school too at grade seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also-- and who-- who did you go to cheder with? Your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sisters go too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, my sisters went already to gymnasium like the high school. They were, and they graduated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me-- tell me what your day was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got up early in the morning like children. You know, we always want to play. To go to school, nobody wanted to go. We want to play. We had to go. So we went to school. Some children-- they had something to eat. So he'd eat a little bit at home-- nothing-- nothing to eat at home. We went away with a hungry stomach. How can you learn with an empty stomach?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to school. We learned to 11 o'clock. At 11 o'clock, we were supposed to go home, a little soup. So we didn't-- some didn't have it. So there'll be some rabbis-- like, we brought our breakfast. Some children brought breakfast. Like I brought I figured out two pieces of bread. So the rabbi took everything out together. And-- and he gave for every child a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he made a-- a bank-- money collected for milk-- five pennies. Every-- so there was only class, the rabbi. He's here in the picture too. And the rest didn't have it. It was very, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were very poor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very poor. Were you from-- what kind of family did you-- did you have? Did you have more money in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of a home did you live in? Did you live in a house or an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have our own houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: A house.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how many rooms were in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my-- where we lived, we had a whole house bigger like this. And-- and down in the front, we had another house which stores that  my father rented out. Over there, we-- my sister had a-- she sold-- sold beer, like a tavern. And in the back of the house was a few rooms. We lived over there too. We-- we had-- we were comfortable-- not, I mean, rich, rich, richer-- comfortable. Very comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: In comparison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two maids-- a Jewish one, you know, and a gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Let's go back to-- to the Jewish community. What other organizations were in-- were in Ciechanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], the Betar, the Bund.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you involved in any of the organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was small. Yes, a little bit. No, but not-- not actually involved, involved. Yes, it was a small city. So where are you gonna go? Your girlfriend goes. So you go-- your girlfriend. And so you have 10, 12 here-- the same-- the same girls where we went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you believe in Israel? Were-- did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a Zionist? Did you ever think you would get to Israel? Did you want to as a young person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- but yes. We made a big mistake when Jabotinsky came to our city. And they begged us. [CRYING] He said, Jews, you have got nothing to do here. We don't want you here. You better move out. So what happened? The fathers find out that we were over there listening to him. We came home, and we got a spanking for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did Jabotinsky come? Can you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. One second. I think it was in 19-- in the '30s. 1930 like that or '29, Jabotinsky came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father wasn't a Zionist. So he got very upset with you for going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my father-- everybody. Everybody-- all the Jews didn't want to go. [CRYING] Even though with the Germans, the Germans came into our city and said we should run away because Hitler doesn't like us. Not-- so nobody wants that anyway. He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. If you're born here, we're gonna die here. So that's what happens if they took us to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler rose to power in 1933--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did it affect your life in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember how it affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we couldn't go-- we were afraid to go. 90% was [INAUDIBLE]. So they were even bigger after-- after that. A Jewish fellow couldn't get a license, or liquor, or tobacco, sugar-- you couldn't get a license for that. Even we had two trucks like cartage trucks. You couldn't even get the license for it. You had to go to a German for that. You pay him so much a month. Because he had the license, we could use his license. Like, here, here are some trucks. Hoffman has  owned. Passman has owned. Hoffman is dead a long time ago. But he's got the license. You use his license and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This was  in 1933, 1934?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1933 after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about Nazism at the time? Did-- did your family talk about Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never could I-- did America know it was going to happen like this? Did you believe that they were going to burn live people? Live children?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- let's go to September 1, 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, we had trucks. So we knew a couple days before the airplanes come. So everybody said, don't worry. That's ours, the Polacks. That's ours, ours. We gonna kill the Germans in no time. So in the meantime, the Germans was already in the city. We took the trucks. And we ran away to Warsaw. When we come to Warsaw on the road, the Polacks stopped us. They were already-- they were not Polacks, Polacks. They were half German like Volkdeutsche. I don't know what you call them here. You call them here Volksdeutsche. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: German descent.

speaker: interviewee
text: German descent. And finally we got to Warsaw. And we-- I was over there for six days. Then I don't know if you remember, there used to be a General Sikorski, Sikorski, general. And he come the 6th of September. He said, all Jewish young people should run away. So I took the-- the truck, and we had a family. I was supposed to go to a near city. And I took the truck with a family. I don't know if you heard, but they are a very rich family-- Polanski. They were born in Lodz. Like here, we would say, [INAUDIBLE]. I took them. And we ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And soon, we came at the bridge. We had to go to another side of Warsaw past the-- past the [INAUDIBLE], you know, [INAUDIBLE]. You know, they had a-- what you call it? Like here, Ontario, a-- a lake. So we had to-- so they-- but they bombed the bridge. They bombed.

speaker: interviewee
text: They bombed the bridge. Anyway, I couldn't go through. And we got to another little city. In the little city, we went far-- far to-- to Bialystok. That's another city. Bialystok was already-- the-- the Polish Army. They tried to make an army come back. In the meantime, the Germans came and bombed it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we ran away as far right straight to the border, Russian-Polish border. That border at that time was Rovno. That-- between-- I don't know if you've heard of  the city Kobryn where [INAUDIBLE] was born and-- and another city, Terespol, Kovel, Lodz, the little cities that we pressed through never these airplanes. So we came to Rovno, and they said, OK, here we gonna stop. And here, we're going to get everybody-- every Polish from the army together. We're gonna go back, fight the German. In the meantime, Germans come and threw bombs and explode the whole city because they-- they-- they brought everything to-- to Rovno-- the tanks, with the guns, with everything. You know. And that was exactly 15 days the war was done-- 16, or 17 days, exactly on Yom Kippur-- Kippur day. I remember there were children. We didn't have nothing to eat. So we bought a goose. So who is going to kill it? So we went to a shochet. The shochet killed the-- the goose. And we all eat it for Yom Kippur. Then the-- the Russians come in. So a few days later, the Russians come in. And that's when we got with the Russians. I actually didn't see the Germans before when I got back to Poland, because I smuggled the-- the border back from-- like the Russians straightened out the border. The Germans were already [INAUDIBLE]. And the Russians took it back, because they [INAUDIBLE] half Poland and half-- half Russian-- half, I mean, German took it. So I came to [INAUDIBLE], I couldn't go farther. And from over there, they were all together with small children. But what-- what-- what we going to do over there? So I smuggled-- smuggled the border back to Poland twice to bring my mother back.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't want to go. My uncle didn't want to go. Nobody wants to go. So they all got-- they all went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go back to Ciechanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got from Ciechanow to Auschwitz when I back with mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a ghetto in Ciechanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What-- no, there was a [? known ?] place. But it was not a [? burg ?] like in Lodz. All right. And that city was around the ghetto. We couldn't make it, because they demolished all the Jewish houses. They demolished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so where are they gonna put us? So they put in-- in the barns. There was-- like for the whole city, [INAUDIBLE]. They start-- so we lived in those places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they herd you in with how many people in a barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: As many as could go in. What do you mean, how many? Who counts? We had over 100 in our yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, in this barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole time after '42 when they-- when they evacuate us too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you were evacuated to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they-- they take you to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: By cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: The cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I got that here in the book too. So they come, like, I don't know. You know. There was a little like here, you got [INAUDIBLE]. There was a conference at that time-- Hitler with Goebbels, Goering, you know, Hess-- they all-- they all went-- Hossler and Koch. So they all-- they got-- they all got together. And they called and went for a conference in a villa not far from Berlin. They called it Wannsee. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] He thought it was the solution to try-- to kill all the Jews. There was Eichmann at that time, Rosenberg, Waldheim, I guess. Waldheim, I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go-- let's go back to-- they-- they herded you. And then they put you on the cattle cars to go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the cattle car. How many were in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewer
text: 100 in the cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: 100 in the cattle car. You sat like that first. Any-- the young fellows like me I had a position already. I drove a big truck, because like we had trucks. I learned myself to back up the truck like that. So when I come to Auschwitz, turn towards home back, so a gendarme came out in the marketplace because [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You know what that means? Every Jew that was in the city has to come out on the marketplace. If not, you'll be killed. They'll shoot you right away. If they went to  a house, and they find somebody, they shoot you right away right on the spot-- not a-- not such a thing, but, yes, shoot you right away. So I told mother I'm going. She started to cry, don't go. Don't go. So I said, I have to go. If they catch me, it's no good anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I come to the marketplace. And I see everybody from the SS, the gendarme, everybody come catch a few Jews and take them away-- a few Jews and take them away from the market. I liked to clean the toilets over there and go shopping for-- dig-- dig for a garden like that. That was a good job already. So all of a sudden, a gendarme comes. Like, Gendarme means a policeman, but not the city police-- from the-- from the outside city police. Like, here, you could say you've got how many types of police you got here? The Mounted, and you've got the city police, and you've got another kind of police. So he came to me and says he needs a mechanic. So I lift up my hand. He needs a mechanic. So he comes to me and says, listen, you are a mechanic? Yes. Come with me. I said, how can I  come? I got to be here. You come with me. He worked-- he was the-- like the help-- not the helper. Like he was like a-- like we had a maid. He was the adjutant to help the-- to be a big man over there, gendarme, not-- not SS. So-- so I told him I'm a  mechanic. So he took me to-- to over there not too far was a-- the city hall. It was a little hill down, because-- had-- we had electricity like a turbine to make electricity. So you have to have where the water goes. So he took me over there. And when comes home, he [? showed me ?] I should take a battery. He asked what's the battery? That's a mechanic. He said, he wanted the battery put a car. He had an old car. So I asked him where are we going? So he told me we were going here and here. He was-- actually, I must say, I worked for him. He said to me, here and here-- [INAUDIBLE] with our neighborhood. The next house. So I come over there. He said he had to go bring the keys up to the big building, you know, where the offices were over there. So we went over there. In the meantime, I put in the battery-- positive to positive and negative to negative. He asked me how I know. I said, listen, I told you I am a mechanic. So he said to me, don't go any place tomorrow. You'll come right here. You're gonna wash the car. You're going to work for us. I said, how can I do it? I can't do it. I have to have a piece of paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, don't worry. The head man is going to come right down. The head man came down. He talked a little Polish. He was in the First World-- the First War-- he was in the same city, because we lived right on the border. All of Poland was-- was like a border with-- with German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he comes to me and starts to talk to me in Polish. He knew a few Jews already from the First War. The Jews were not over there anymore. They were killed. So he said, listen, I had-- my man, his name was Kandi-- Kandi. And they told him you should stay here. He refused. I said, I don't know if he has nothing. I have to have something. If I don't have a paper, they are gonna come and catch me anyway. He said, don't worry. You'll come to me. So in the meantime, he started the car, and I went with him. We went around on the-- in the villages, a little-- a little like to the gentiles. They had the farm. If you own a horse, if you own a car, two pigs, and like that, they collect it away from them. So we were talking on the way-- the way home. He said, listen, don't go away. I want to give you something. So he gave me a package of tobacco. And he called the  men that they should never have let me go. And he should give me something too. And that was it. I stayed over there for a few months, because they took him away, and the SS come. After them, the SS come. The SS took it over-- the [INAUDIBLE], the black ones. You know. And I stayed over there. It was the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] is-- where the court is. What do you call the building? Not the city hall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the city hall, but the court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where the court is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like the court. What do you call that building?

speaker: interviewer
text: The courthouse. Like a courthouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Courthouse. What do you call the building? The municipality building? What do you call that?

speaker: interviewer
text: That sounds good, municipality building.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to it. So-- then  he called me into his office. He start to talk to me. There was another fellow. He had two fellows like that. Another was Freitag. I remember like that. They were-- they were OK. They were OK. And so then-- then when they came back from Wannsee-- that was the solution.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's when they-- you-- you go-- went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went on the cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were on-- in the cattle car, did you see people? Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they see you? Did they want to help you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, if you-- if I could have jumped down-- maybe, I wouldn't know, because there was a soldier in the back and a soldier in the front. So some even broke the-- broke the floor for the cattle car. And if they catch you, the Poles give you away. You didn't have to go over there. If that would be the case, you could, I wouldn't say, much-- maybe another 20% will be alive. You see-- you know, Judy what happened in Kielce, the pogrom right after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the conditions on the train. Was there any water? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Absolutely nothing. Nothing. Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a trip was it for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip? Two nights and three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it in the evening or in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Evening. Saturday evening. I remember, evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the day you arrived? The night you arrived what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date when I left, I think, the 7th-- I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I think it would have been the 7th or the 9th of December, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened as you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, right away we had a doctor with us-- he took the poison, the pill. He poisoned himself right away. He saw there-- we didn't know. Who knows? Auschwitz. We didn't-- they fooled us. They said-- they said that they take us to work, like the parents and the-- the children are going to stay in the houses and the barracks. And we were going to work. And we come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right away, as soon as they opened the barracks-- the-- the cattle wagons-- so there's pictures in the book. You can see it. They opened the cattle wagons, started with the-- the sticks hitting. And you would never believe it. They killed right away on this. So who knows? So we stayed-- I remember like that with the same men who was in the city how we select a first transport. A guy was in the transport [INAUDIBLE] people, 1,000 people. So 1,000 people, you need only 10-- 10-- 10-- 10-- 10 cattle cars. So for the 1,000 people-- then they put in another 500 second transport for another city. So they had-- before us was the Holland people. They were in worse like me, because they didn't know the language. They didn't know nothing. And they had I remember the mukluks, the wooden shoes. And you couldn't work in them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was very good them shoes, because you didn't freeze at your feet. So finally, Mengele stayed like that-- a little stick here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he picked out from our 1,000 men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He picked out I would say about 155, 160 people-- the young-- the youngest, the strongest. With me exactly, and I went where the mother is. I wasn't going by myself. And all of a sudden, I come-- get  a kick in my back. And I see a German at the back. He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You know what I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't stay here. Go here. So I went over there on the other side. [? That ?] with the life. [INAUDIBLE] the other way [INAUDIBLE]. Lots of people were screaming they wanted to go over to the car with the red cross. Always the red cross behind, always with the trucks on the front and on the back with the red cross, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the doctor poisoned himself, so we know a little bit what's happened. We got in in the camp. I got in-- it was nighttime. We didn't know and where right away, where-- where we went was right away with canes. They hit you with the sticks. You would never believe it. So then we went over there. And we stayed in that camp in a block, I think, I remember the wooden, [INAUDIBLE]. We stayed over there till the morning. The next morning we come. Mr. Silber, I think we're going to take a little break right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's no problem.
